title: Fudgy brownies
subtitle: Dense, intensely chocolatey brownies with a crackled top
excerpt: Fudgy brownies have more chocolate and fat and less flour than cakey ones. The crackled shiny top comes from beating the eggs and sugar together until the mixture ribbons before adding the melted chocolate. Pull them from the oven when the edges are set and the centre still has a wobble.
sourceNotes: Brownies originated in American domestic cookery in the late nineteenth century, first documented in Fannie Farmer's Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896). They became widely popular in British baking through the mid-twentieth century. The fudgy style (high fat, low flour, underbaked relative to a cakey brownie) is the contemporary preference in British and American home baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Cut and wrap individually. Freeze for up to 2 months. Defrost at room temperature for 1 hour.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Brownies improve after 12 hours — bake the day before and leave in the tin to cool completely. The fudgy texture firms up and improves overnight.

glossaryTerms.definition: The point in brownie making when the eggs and sugar have been beaten long enough that the mixture flows off the whisk in a thick, pale ribbon that holds its shape briefly before dissolving back. Reaching this stage aerates the mixture and creates the characteristic crackled, shiny surface on the baked brownie. Under-beating produces a matte, dull top.

content.text: The shiny, crackled top of a good brownie requires the eggs and sugar to be beaten to the
(pale, thick, and trailing off the whisk in ribbons) before any chocolate is added. For the fudgy texture, the centre should still wobble when you shake the tin. It will firm as it cools.

Heat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper. Melt {{dark-chocolate}} and {{unsalted-butter}} together in a bowl over barely simmering water, or in the microwave in 30-second bursts, stirring between each. Leave to cool for 5 minutes. In a separate large bowl, beat {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} with an electric whisk for 3–4 minutes until pale and thickened to the ribbon stage. Beat in {{vanilla-extract}}.

Pour the cooled chocolate mixture into the egg mixture and fold together gently — don't beat. Sift in {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, and {{salt-table}} and fold again until just combined. Pour into the tin and bake for 22–25 minutes until the edges are set and the centre still has a slight wobble when the tin is shaken. A skewer 2 cm from the edge should come out with a few moist crumbs. Leave to cool completely in the tin before cutting, at least 1 hour.
